steps.title: Identify the structure on each side
steps.content: Left: "This 2013 exhibition celebrated the diverse achievements" — an independent clause ending mid-prepositional phrase. Right: "artists of Latin American descent" — the object of the preposition of. The blank must supply just the preposition to complete the phrase.

steps.title: Apply the rule: no punctuation separates a preposition from its object
steps.content:
A preposition connects directly to its object. Any punctuation between them breaks the phrase.
  • Choice A — ‘of;’: "achievements of; artists..." — a semicolon after a preposition creates a fragment on each side. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘of:’: "achievements of: artists..." — a colon after a preposition is never grammatical; colons follow independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘of,’: "achievements of, artists..." — a comma cannot interrupt a prepositional phrase between the preposition and its object. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘of’: "achievements of artists of Latin American descent" — the preposition attaches cleanly to its object, no punctuation needed. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
